speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 13th. We are currently interviewing Irene Boris. My name is Clifford Davids, and this is tape number Four. Mrs. Boris, you had moved to Ulm. How long were you living at Ulm for?

speaker: interviewee
text: '46 till '49. The beginning of '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working for the UNRRA.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: U-N-R-A, UNRRA.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that is the Jewish organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was the-- I guess the national organization. Then the highest was the Jewish organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: So they worked together. And I was the secretary. I use to write out some-- they used to call them vouchers, for clothes, but [INAUDIBLE]. And I worked there till the beginning of '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother was with you at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was-- this time, she was very sick. My mother, when she came-- in Berlin, she had a little stroke, which she recovered fine. And then when we came to Ulm, she became paralyzed. And she was in the hospital till-- till '48, paralyzed completely. She didn't feel anything the legs up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And thanks, I have to say to the German nuns, who owned-- You know, they were working in the hospital, and they gave you the attention that she would never get. And one day-- then they send her to [? Heidelberg ?] and they made, like, oh, what do you call it? Like a form there. She was laying down, special, you know? And they didn't give me no hopes. And I says, what I'm going to do with a paralyzed woman, and me, a young girl who doesn't know anything? And I was just, you know, I gave up. I says, I'm going to survive the way I can.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day I came in, and this was-- they transferred her back to Ulm. I came to Ulm in the doctor, in the morning, when they visits there. So he said, look at what I see Mrs. Rozenfeld can feel it. He put a needle in her legs and she jumped-- her leg jumped.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says-- what-- Mrs. Rozenfeld had feelings. And she was, again, there many months. I don't remember even how long. And she start walking, and she was never sick again till she had a stroke in the '80s. Would you believe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: She worked very hard here. She attended my kids. I mean, I worked and she was with the kids. And doing all kind of cooking, and baking, and partying, and catering to everyone. And she never was sick till she got, again, a stroke in the '80s.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the years that you were living in Ulm, what did you find out about what had happened to, not only your brothers, but to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. You took it so lightly. Because it's just that, you know, everybody was so occupied with nothing, with survival, and having more food, another dress, with having-- going to a movie, going to opera. You know, something that you forgot about the things. You just-- It's another day. Nobody talked about-- I mean, you felt it, because, you know, this is not [? hell. ?] Like I thought about my brother. You know, the communication wasn't-- you didn't had-- You didn't have the [? heart ?] to have a communication. I had with a few from my city in the-- in the States. They [INAUDIBLE] me. And I-- like I already mentioned, when I wanted to go from Russia back home to see who survived, who is there, I wrote a letter to somebody who used to work for us, for the guy who wanted, I should stay with them. And he says, better don't come, because they'll kill you the first. As soon you come, they'll kill you. Because, he says, my brother was in the [INAUDIBLE]. That's what they called the organization. And he was the one who took everything away from your house and everything. So if you'll come, you'll be the first one to get killed from him. Nobody [? knew ?] him, because he was hiding still in the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he says, don't come, better. If you want to come, go to a big city, like Rowne or some other bigger city. But don't come to the city of [INAUDIBLE], Kostopol. And then I gave up. I didn't want to even go. But then I wrote to another one, and they sent me a letter that the house, everything is gone. The house is no more there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the ghetto. They burned it after the [INAUDIBLE], they burned it, and nobody survived. And then, would you believe, I stopped asking, even. I just gave up. I concentrated on myself, my mother, and my brother. It was from the big family, cousins, uncles, didn't-- didn't mean anything. Like, I didn't even mention my grandmother. I mean, she-- I mean, we left her. She says, oh, I'm too old. I'm not going to go with you. And things happened that, you know, you didn't-- you don't think anymore. I didn't want to think about nobody. I just consecrated on the few living one. And I don't know why. Maybe some people are different, but that's the way I felt. If they dead, what I'm gonna start, you know, going to the [? place. ?] Now, in Florida, there is somebody from my city, and he's younger than I am, two years. And he's a wealthy man, and a nice guy. And he has, every year, the few leftovers coming up to his house. And we always have something to say. And one, he's a-- his father was a [? hassan, ?] a [INAUDIBLE]. And he has a beautiful voice. So he always makes something like a little memorial for the-- mentions all the names. And they've wrote a book, and we sent-- everybody sent some money, and they wrote everybody's name. And then, we sent some money now to-- to [INAUDIBLE]. His sister lives there, and she took on herself to bury every bone she'll finded. She wouldn't go away till she would bury it. She was in the partisans. Her husband was Ukrainian, and he saved her. She married him, and she lived. She has two sons now. They're in Israel. And she took on herself that she finds out more now, the people were killed, and she goes around and picks up bones, whatever, and she buries them. And she puts a [INAUDIBLE] on every place. Like from Kostopol and the little towns around. So she puts it, a [INAUDIBLE]. So we send her money, and I have the video from there. And two people came in, one from Israel and one who survived. I don't know where he is now. And they tried to-- She says, if-- she'll bury all the bones she can find, because now, she pays the Ukrainian, some of them, and they still, you know, they tell her [INAUDIBLE] there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So soon, she'll put up some memorial, so she'll-- you know, she'll go away from there. What happened to Abraham?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham died here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you reunited with him back in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, in--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Ulm?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ulm. He came a few weeks after I came here. He came with my mother. I was married in 1949. And then, I became pregnant, I guess.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- so you know that they took me to Munich. And over there, I waited-- They supposed to give us planes, because I was too far gone. So they gave us plane to come-- planes to come here. Just happened, they had three planes. And I said, what's the difference? So I went on the third plane. Never been off. One belt, and that's it. So I had to stay there till I wrote a letter to my aunts here. And they sent out $850, a ticket from the-- this was Pan American Airlines, and they were poor people, like I said.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were just working people. And they sent out, and my husband and I, we encountered anti-Semitism over there. The [? one ?] [? they ?] [? were ?] [? there ?] in camp, they were mostly Yugoslavian, mostly Yugoslavian, and probably Polacks. I don't know exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet your husband in Ulm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I met my husband in Berlin, but we went away like two young kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was refugee as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: From? Where was he from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he came to-- to Ulm then with his uncles. And then we met again, and we got married. So in Munich, he and I have to go to the United States. And they were so mean, the guard, just shoving their-- whatever they had in their heart, they'd show it. And it was mostly, because my husband wanted to have a fight with them, because he was a strong 6-foot-1 or whatever. He was by that time a big man. And I was holding his hands and I begged them not to go into a fight with him, because otherwise I wouldn't be able to-- in-- I'm supposed to have, a few weeks later, my baby. So things weren't so-- and then they flew from Munich to-- [INAUDIBLE] someplace in Germany. And from over there, we took another plane. Took me 24 hours to come here in '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you give birth to your first child here in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Three weeks after I came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me-- Let me, again, backtrack. Did your family ever consider going over to United States sometime in the-- in the late '30s or early '40s?

speaker: interviewee
text: The thing is that my mother had papers to come to the States, together with her sisters. But just because my father was sick, they didn't let her go, and she was married by that time. They were single. So all her family immigrated here in the early '20s or in the-- '18 or so. And she was the only one left over there, because she got married, and my father was a sick man by the time already.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you and your husband come to United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came here August, August 14. Matter of fact, August 14, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you settle down?

speaker: interviewee
text: We settled right in Dorchester.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you live in Dorchester for?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left-- we lived in Dorchester, then '49. Well, took us five-- till-- Well, we moved to Mattapan. We bought a house in Mattapan. And then from [INAUDIBLE], we moved in here in the early '60s I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here is Brooklyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that you'd like to say that we either haven't covered in the interview or any remarks that you'd like to make in conclusion?

speaker: interviewee
text: The thing is, I want to say, my husband regretted, always, coming here. He wanted to go to Israel. But just because my family was here, I insisted coming here. Things weren't good and weren't bad. We worked very hard here, my husband and I. But we accomplished a lot. We accomplished having educated kids. I had respect for my mother. She lived with me. I supported my-- my brother all the years. And-- like, I went here to school. I worked first in a clothing factory for years. Then I took civil service and I worked for the state, for the Register of Motor Vehicles, I worked, 10 years. Then, when my mother became very ill with the stroke and so, I figured, she needs me, and I didn't feel so good. So I just quit my work. And I says, enough is enough. I'll devote the rest of my life to my mother more. I owe my life to her. [SOBBING] Well, that's what I did, and I'm very proud. My family respects me for this. My kids respect me.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I say to my kids, what's gonna be my old age? They says, Ma, what did you do to your mother? Did you give-- give away your mother to old age home or so? Yeah, my mother was [INAUDIBLE]. So this is my biggest reward, what I have for my work. And I think I have. And the rest is history. So things were going bad. There's certain days I may be more moody than-- than I'm supposed to be. Maybe I'm a little bit more complaining than I'm supposed to complain. Demanding from my kids more than they can give me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it's something that I always think, I gave it to them, so I'm-- I'm a demanding mother more than maybe I-- but I get it. I get respect. They include me in everything. And even when my English wasn't good, I went to-- they took me to the library, and my daughter always said before, my mother has a-- such a cute accent. She didn't want, maybe, somebody will say something, so she warned them. And they were-- They're always proud of me. They were never ashamed, never. Never [? stood. ?] And I have to say that, even that when my husband died, his daughter, Sandy, had the eulogy, said-- my son was different, but my daughter, Sandy, she says, my father was a meat cutter. She didn't mention that, you know, he was [INAUDIBLE] and the selling insurance and this, and he made a living from real estate. But she says, my father was a poor meat cutter, working for [? firms ?] [? later say ?] and he accomplished everything by hard, honest work. And she says, I have to say, he was a honest, honest man, and a good father. And that's the memory I have. My mother was a honest person, never insulted anybody. And she had a good life, living with the kids, with the grandchildren. and she saw them all getting married. She was to all four of them. She went to the weddings, and then she died. The rabbi said, she had-- she had-- she started a book, and she accomplished. She finished the book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to give us the names of your children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son, his name is Larry Leibel. That's after my father's-- after my husband's father, because he thought he's the only one. My father had names. And then comes Janet, and she is [INAUDIBLE]. And then comes Sandy, and she's named after my father, Symcha, Symcha. And my son has one son, Shane. And my Sandy has three kids-- Gabriel, [? Jacqueline, ?] and Emily. Janet has no kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm thankful to God for everything he gave me. He gave me life, and that's all. Hard, hard life. Till now, even I could enjoy it more than I enjoy it, which I do enjoy more than other people. But I still thinks it's not enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always remember the past. I believe in charity. I believe in helping people as much as I can.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's all. So my life is accomplished. I feel this way, OK? Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mrs. Boris, would you like to introduce these two lovely ladies standing next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my-- I have twin daughters. This is my older daughter, Janet. We call her older, because she was born first.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my baby, Sandy. She was born seven-- seven minutes later. What else can I say about them? All the good things. I haven't got nothing bad. Sandy, would you like to say a few words?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would just like to thank you all, and everyone else involved in this project. I think it's going to be very valuable for our future as a historical document of our past. As the mother of three children, I think it's really important to document all that's happened so they have a history to grow up with, and to work with, and to appreciate, with the hopes that they will feel their Judaism as adults will stay with them and will be as important to them as it is to all of us. And the history was just a tragedy. And it's important that we all remember that happened and makes us who we are today. And I think-- I know my mom talks a lot about her past. My dad really didn't. But it has made them into the people they are, and has given us the strength that we have.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's about it. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd just like to express my appreciation for your work as well as my mother's to be a [SOBBING] modern woman in America, yet to hold on to traditional values that has given us our growth and education. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Would you like to have any last words?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I want to say I have my oldest son. His name is Larry. He was born here, three weeks after I came to the United States. He lives now in Denver, otherwise he would be here. And he has his family in Denver.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mrs. Boris, and thank you both.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
